coredata.dc:title: In vitro detection of iron overload in leukemia cells using o-phenylenediamine-derived carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Faleke, H.O.
Pappas, D.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanoparticles have emerged as highly effective sensors for detecting metal ions within cells in recent years. Phenylenediamine is well known as a starting material used to synthesize carbon dot nanoparticles. This study investigates the synthesis and application of o-PD CDs derived from o-phenylenediamine using a microwave method, focusing on their selectivity towards ferric iron (Fe³⁺) and their potential as fluorescent probes in leukemia cell lines. Physical and chemical characteristics of the oPD CDs show that the surface of the nanoparticles has redox-active functional groups and an average size of 7.6 nm. The fluorescence properties depict a characteristic absorption peak at 290 nm and 420 nm and maximum fluorescence emission at 570 nm. Initial selectivity experiments confirmed the specificity of o-PD CDs towards Fe³ ⁺, correlating with the cellular storage of excess iron in its ferric state. The fluorescence intensity of o-PD CDs was evaluated in leukemia cells (HL-60 and Jurkat) incubated with ferrous ammonium sulfate (FAS). Results demonstrated a concentration-dependent decrease in fluorescence with increasing iron concentration. Notably, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed in HL-60 cells treated with higher FAS concentrations (200 and 400 µM), suggesting a potential relationship with cell death. HL-60 cells were exposed to varying ethanol concentrations to explore this phenomenon and analyzed via flow cytometry following incubation with o-PD CDs. Comparative analysis of the fluorescence profiles between HL-60 and Jurkat cells revealed a distinct difference, attributed to the insusceptibility of Jurkat cells to ferroptosis, a form of iron-dependent cell death, unlike HL-60 cells. These findings suggest that o-PD CDs can serve as effective fluorescent probes for detecting iron-related cellular changes and highlight their potential utility in distinguishing between different leukemia cell lines based on their susceptibility to ferroptosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: O-phenylenediamine
Carbon dots
Iron overload
HL-60 cells
Jurkat cells
Ferroptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the o-phenylenediamine carbon dots
Preparation of buffers with varying pH values
Sensitivity and iron selectivity of oPD CDs
Detection of iron overload by oPD CDs
pH sensitivity and iron selectivity of oPD CDs
Cellular Imaging and in-vitro iron overload detection

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical characterization of the oPD CDs. (a) Representative TEM image of oPD CDs. (b)Size distribution of oPD CDs.

Chemical characterization of the oPD CDs. (a) Zeta potential box plot of oPD CDs; (b) FTIR spectra of the oPD CDs showing the functional groups present; (c) SEM-EDX spectra showing the elemental composition of the oPD.

Optical properties of oPD CDs. (a) Orange line and axes: UV–VIS absorption spectra of oPD CDs; blue lines and axes: fluorescence profile of the oPD CDs; (b) excitation-independent fluorescent emission of oPD CDs from 420 nm to 520 nm).

(a) Effect of pH on the oPD CDs fluorescence intensity; (b) Effect of different ions and amino acids on the oPD CDs fluorescence intensity; (c) Linear curve of oPD CDs fluorescence against increasing concentrations of Fe3+.

Cytotoxicity of oPD CDs. (a) Effect of varying concentrations of the oPD CDs on cell toxicity; (b) Effect of 0.09 mg/ml oPD CDs incubation time on the cell toxicity. Significance levels are denoted as follows: P ≤ 0.05 marked with *, P ≤ 0.01 marked with **, P ≤ 0.001 marked with ***, and P ≤ 0.0001 marked with ****.

Cell imaging of oPD CDs. (a) HL 60 cells viewed under blue filter cube after incubating with oPD CDs; (b) Merged image of the four a-d filter cubes; (c) Image plot profile of the a-d filter cubes images. Scale bar = 20μm.

In-vitro iron overload detection. (a) mean gray value of ∼100 HL 60 cells stained with oPD CDs after treating with ferrous ammonium sulfate (FAS); (b) flow cytometry mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) of HL 60 cells probed with oPD CDs after administering varying concentrations of FAS; (c) MFI of Jurkat cells probed with oPD CDs after treating with different concentrations of FAS; (d)) mean gray value of ∼100 HL 60 cells stained with oPD CDs after treating with FAS at different time. Significance levels are denoted as follows: P ≤ 0.05 marked with *, P ≤ 0.01 marked with **, P ≤ 0.001 marked with ***, and P ≤ 0.0001 marked with ****.
(a) microscopy image of oPD CD-treated HL 60 cells (b) microscopy image of oPD CD-treated HL 60 cells incubated with FAS (c) Image plot profile of (a and b).

Fluorescence detection of ethanol-induced cell death by oPD CDs. (a) MFI of HL 60 cells probed with oPD CDs after treatment with ethanol (b) Image profile of microscopy images in SI Fig. 5. Significance levels are denoted as follows: P ≤ 0.05 marked with *, P ≤ 0.01 marked with **, P ≤ 0.001 marked with ***, and P ≤ 0.0001 marked with ****.
Previous studies on iron-sensing carbon dots synthesized from o-phenylenediamine as a core starting material or an N-doping agent to other starting materials.

row.entry: High sensitivity and selectivity with a low detection limit of 22.1 nM for Fe³ ⁺.
Suitable for real-time and on-site detection using paper strip and aerogel-based sensors.
Enable ratiometric Fe³ ⁺ detection using the fluorescence intensity ratio (F350/F570)
High quantum yield (31.5 %), good photostability and pH stability
The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.177 μM, allowing for trace-level Fe³ ⁺ detection.
Detect Fe³ ⁺ in real water samples without interference from other metal ions
Fe³ ⁺ detection with dual-emission properties, photostability, and an “ON-OFF-ON” switching mechanism.
Leukemia cell line (HL−60) and T lymphocyte (Jurkat) cell line
Sensitive for detecting Fe3+ in Leukemia cells

head.ce:title: In vitro detection of iron overload in leukemia cells using o-phenylenediamine-derived carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Texas Tech University, Lubbock, TX 79409-1061, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Texas Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Texas Tech University, Lubbock, TX 79409-1061

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanoparticles have emerged as highly effective sensors for detecting metal ions within cells in recent years. Phenylenediamine is well known as a starting material used to synthesize carbon dot nanoparticles. This study investigates the synthesis and application of o-PD CDs derived from o-phenylenediamine using a microwave method, focusing on their selectivity towards ferric iron (Fe³⁺) and their potential as fluorescent probes in leukemia cell lines. Physical and chemical characteristics of the oPD CDs show that the surface of the nanoparticles has redox-active functional groups and an average size of 7.6 nm. The fluorescence properties depict a characteristic absorption peak at 290 nm and 420 nm and maximum fluorescence emission at 570 nm. Initial selectivity experiments confirmed the specificity of o-PD CDs towards Fe³ ⁺, correlating with the cellular storage of excess iron in its ferric state. The fluorescence intensity of o-PD CDs was evaluated in leukemia cells (HL-60 and Jurkat) incubated with ferrous ammonium sulfate (FAS). Results demonstrated a concentration-dependent decrease in fluorescence with increasing iron concentration. Notably, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed in HL-60 cells treated with higher FAS concentrations (200 and 400 µM), suggesting a potential relationship with cell death. HL-60 cells were exposed to varying ethanol concentrations to explore this phenomenon and analyzed via flow cytometry following incubation with o-PD CDs. Comparative analysis of the fluorescence profiles between HL-60 and Jurkat cells revealed a distinct difference, attributed to the insusceptibility of Jurkat cells to ferroptosis, a form of iron-dependent cell death, unlike HL-60 cells. These findings suggest that o-PD CDs can serve as effective fluorescent probes for detecting iron-related cellular changes and highlight their potential utility in distinguishing between different leukemia cell lines based on their susceptibility to ferroptosis.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon nanoparticles have been reported as sensors for metal ions in cells [1]. Their nano-dimensional structure (∼10–100 nm) allows them to penetrate the cell’s plasma membrane and interact with molecules such as nucleic acids, proteins, metal ions, and sugar moieties [2]. The presence of specific functional groups retained from the source of the starting materials also facilitates an active transport of the nanoparticle via specific related receptors on the plasma membrane [3]. In addition to the size and chemical properties, the optical properties of these carbon dots nanoparticles also contribute to their bioimaging applications [4]. Some carbon dot nanoparticles have demonstrated the ability to serve as biosensors and modulate their fluorescence properties in the presence of different metal ions, such as iron, copper, and arsenate [5–7]. Phenylenediamine, well known as a starting material for synthesizing several heterocycle compounds, is one of the popular starting materials used to synthesize carbon dots nanoparticles. The choice of phenylenediamine tends to be associated with the presence of the amino groups that contribute to the production of oxidative N-doped carbon dots nanoparticles [8]. Its ortho, meta, and para isomer derivatives have been used to synthesize carbon dots with differing fluorescence properties for various applications [9]. Among these three isomers, the ortho isomer, o-phenylenediamine, has mostly been used singly or in addition to other compounds to synthesize carbon dots nanoparticles that sense Fe2+/Fe3+ ions. A similar approach was reportedly employed to make iron and hydrogen peroxide-sensing and bioimaging carbon dots [10]. However, we established analytical information on iron detection in this study. Table 1 summarizes iron-sensing carbon dots synthesized from o-phenylenediamine as a core starting material or an N-doping agent to other starting materials, highlighting their application and selected analytical parameters.

Iron is the most abundant biometal in nature, and several proteins utilize it to accomplish their cellular functions, such as oxidative phosphorylation, DNA synthesis, oxygen transport, drug metabolism, and immune functions [11]. Due to iron’s redox-sensitive nature, several mechanisms participate to keep its regulation coordinated. Prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells employ different genetic regulatory elements at all the facets of gene expression, from DNA synthesis to post-translational modification of proteins involved in iron homeostasis to maintain the intracellular concentration of iron [12]. Perturbation in iron homeostasis has resulted in different diseases associated with iron deficiency or overload. Iron overload occurs when free labile iron is accumulated in the cell’s redox-active state (Fe3+), which can participate in the Fenton reaction to generate reactive oxygen radicals, resulting in conditions associated with oxidative stress, such as neurodegenerative diseases [13] and cancer [14].

Detecting iron overload is crucial for understanding and treating diseases associated with dysregulated iron homeostasis, such as hemochromatosis, neurodegenerative disorders, and certain cancers. Several analytical techniques have been employed for iron detection, including mass spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES), spectrophotometry, immuno-based assays, and colorimetric approaches [15]. While these techniques are highly sensitive and specific, they often require expensive equipment and complex sample preparation and are not always suitable for real-time or intracellular studies. In contrast, nanoparticle-based fluorescent probes, such as carbon dots (CDs), offer unique advantages, including ease of synthesis, biocompatibility, and tunable optical properties, making them highly suitable for bioimaging and intracellular sensing applications.

This study employed a facile microwave-assisted method to synthesize o-phenylenediamine-derived carbon dots (oPD CDs), utilizing the nitrogen-doped structure to enhance their optical and chemical properties. The synthesized oPD CDs demonstrated strong selectivity for ferric ions (Fe³⁺) and exhibited a characteristic fluorescence quenching mechanism. We used these CDs to detect ferrous ammonium sulfate (FAS)-induced iron overload in leukemia cell lines (HL-60 and Jurkat). The results revealed cell-type-specific responses to iron overload, where HL-60 cells exhibited a significant increase in fluorescence at higher FAS concentrations, potentially linked to ferroptosis-induced cellular changes. In contrast, Jurkat cells, which are more resistant to ferroptosis, showed a consistent decrease in fluorescence intensity as iron concentrations increased. This differential response highlights the utility of oPD CDs as effective probes for studying iron overload dynamics and ferroptosis in different cellular contexts. These findings highlight the potential of oPD CDs for real-time iron sensing and cellular imaging and provide insights into the underlying mechanisms of iron metabolism in leukemia, paving the way for further applications in disease diagnosis and therapeutic monitoring. In addition, the reporting of novel carbon dot surface chemistries opens new avenues in the discovery and application of new, functional materials.

The chemicals and solvents used in this study are of analytical grade. The precursor o-phenylenediamine (99.5 %) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Boric acid, phosphoric acid, and acetic acid from Fisher Chemical. Ferric chloride (FeCl3, 97 %), ferrous ammonium sulfate ((NH₄)₂Fe(SO₄)₂·6H₂O), Magnesium chloride (MgCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), zinc chloride (ZnCl) were procured from Macron Fine Chemicals L-methionine (L-Met), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), L-valine (L-Val), L-proline (L-Pro), L-tyrosine (L-Tyr) and L-threonine (L-Thr) were secured from States Biochemical Company. L-histidine (L-His, 98 %) was purchased from Thermo Scientific. The phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution was obtained from Mediatech, Inc., and the propidium iodide (PI) was acquired from Molecular Probes Invitrogen Detection Technologies. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was obtained from Mallinckrodt Pharmaceutical Company.

A human leukemia cell line (HL-60) and a human T lymphocyte (Jurkat) cell line procured from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium (Hyclone) containing 1 % penicillin-streptomycin (Sigma-Aldrich) and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Hyclone) and incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2.

The oPD CDs were synthesized using the microwave method, and 25 mg of oPD crystals were dissolved in ultra-pure water and sonicated for 5 min to obtain a precise homogenous mixture. The mixture was transferred into a G30-sized glass vial and subjected to microwave-generated heat at 220°C for 20 mins using an Antor Parr Monowave 400 at 230 psi and ∼150 W. The reaction mixture was allowed to cool and filtered through a 0.20 μm pore Nylon disk filter. The filtrated was freeze-dried and resuspended in 10 ml of ultra-pure water for further experimental procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the o-phenylenediamine carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
An aliquot of the carbon dots solutions was dropped on a carbon grid and viewed using the Hitachi S-7650 transmission electron microscope (TEM) at 60kv. The carbon grid used for the TEM imaging was further used for the scanning electron microscope and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX) data acquisition on a Zeiss Crossbeam 540 with an Oxford EDS system. The dried form of the oPD CDs was used to obtain the attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectrum using the Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 Smart ITR FTIR with an ATR crystal attachment. The quantum yield was calculated using Eqn 1.
(1)
Q
C D
=
Q
R h o
I
C D
A
R h o
η
C D
2
I
R h o
A
C D
η
C D
2
Where Q
CD is the quantum yield of the unknown fluorescent molecule (oPD CDs), QRho is the quantum yield of the reference fluorophore (Rhodamine 110), I
CD is the integrated fluorescence intensity of the unknown sample (oPD CDs), I
Rho is the integrated fluorescence intensity of the reference fluorophore (Rhodamine 110), A
CD is the absorbance of the unknown sample (oPD CDs) at the excitation wavelength, A
CD is the absorbance of the reference fluorophore (Rhodamine 110) at the excitation wavelength,
η
CD
is the refractive index of the solvent (water) used for the unknown sample, and
η
Rho
is the refractive index of the solvent (ethanol) used for the reference fluorophore. The zeta potential of the CDs was also obtained using the Microtrac Stabino particle charge mapping system. The optical characteristics of the CDs were assessed by examining the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and the fluorescence profile of the carbon dots using Agilent 8453 UV-Vis Spectrometer and Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of buffers with varying pH values
ce:section.ce:para: The Britton-Robinson (BR) universal buffer system was used to determine the fluorescence response of the oPD CDs at different pH values. The Britton-Robinson (BR) universal buffer system contains a mixture of 0.04 M boric acid, 0.04 M phosphoric acid, and 0.04 M acetic acid, titrated with 0.2 M sodium hydroxide to obtain varying pH of 2–12. An equal volume of each buffer solution with a respective pH value was mixed with the oPD CD solution before examining the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and iron selectivity of oPD CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the modulatory effects of selected metallic ions on the fluorescence profile of the oPD CDs, 1.0 mM chloride or sulfate salt in 5 % DMSO of Fe3+, Fe2+, NH3+, Zn+, Ca2+, Na+ Mg2+ as well as 1.0 mM concentration of L-Met, L-Phe, L-Val, L-Pro, L-Tyr, L-Thr, L-His as added to oPD CDs solution in a 1:1 ratio. The fluorescence spectra were obtained at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm after the solution was allowed to equilibrate for 5 min. The concentration range of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 μM was used to establish the concentration curve of the effect of ferric ions on the fluorescence of oPD CDs. The detection limit was calculated based on the standard deviation of the response and the slope [21].
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
S
Where σ is the standard deviation of the response, and S is the slope of the calibration curve

The cytotoxicity of the oPD CDs was examined by staining the cells with propidium iodide (PI) after incubating them with cells with varying concentrations of carbon dots and different time frames. HL-60 cells were incubated for 30 min in 0.045, 0.09, 0.18, 0.45, and 0.9 mg/ml oPD CDs solution in PBS for concentration-dependent toxicity. 0.09 mg/ml was examined for its temporal toxicity effect for 1, 2, 4, and 6 hrs. 50 % ethanol was used as a positive control for the toxicity assessment. A total of 10,000 events were collected and analyzed by BD Aria flow cytometer with a 581 nm laser to analyze the fluorescence intensity of the PI, which correlates to the extent of the carbon dot’s cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of iron overload by oPD CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The human leukemia cell line (HL-60) and T lymphocyte (Jurkat) cells were treated for 6 h in varying concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μM) of ferrous ammonium sulfate (FAS) supplemented RMPI media. Afterward, the cells were incubated in 0.09 mg/ml oPD CDs for 30 min, centrifuged, and washed with PBS twice before being analyzed with the BD Arias flow cytometer with a 488 nm laser to analyze the fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticles absorbed by the cells. HL-60 cells were also treated with 100 μM ferrous ammonium sulfate (FAS) supplemented RMPI media at different times (1, 2, 4, 6, and 24 h) and stained with 0.09 mg/ml oPD CDs for 30 min. After incubation, the cells were centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 5 min and washed twice in PBS before analysis using the Nikon Eclipse Ti2 inverted microscope coupled with an sCMOS PCO edge camera and a high-powered LED light source.

To determine the average intensity of cells for each treatment group, the mean intensity per pixel of ∼100 cells was measured using ImageJ. Recorded data were presented as the mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 10.0. Comparisons between means were statistically evaluated by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey’s test. Statistical significance is measured by * for P ≤ 0.05, ** for P ≤ 0.01, *** for P ≤ 0.001 and **** for P ≤ 0.0001.

The successful synthesis of carbon dots was ascertained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as presented in Fig. 1a, showing an abundant quantity of carbon dots. The size distribution of the oPD CDs is depicted in Fig. 1b, which had a size range of 3.8 nm to 17 nm and an average size of 7.6 nm. A bottom-top approach to synthesizing carbon dots involves using starting materials with small molecules, such as o-phenylenediamine, which tend to have smaller sizes than those synthesized from large molecules like protein in a top-bottom approach [3,22]. The size of the oPD CDs obtained from this study shares morphological similarities with carbon dots synthesized by Song et al. [10]. The zeta potential of the oPD CDs after 60 s oscillatory scan shows a −45.25 mV, as shown in Fig. 2a. The zeta potential is dependent on the net surface charge of the nanoparticle, and it depicts the stability of the nanoparticle in suspension as well as how it gets adsorbed [23]. A Zeta potential of ± 30 or more infers particle stability, while particles with a lower zeta potential portray instability and tend to form aggregates. The zeta potential of the obtained oPD CDs shows that the nanoparticle is significantly stable and thus has retained its nano-sizeable morphology for efficient permeabilization of the cell membrane [24].

The functional group present on the surface of the carbon dot nanoparticle was characterized by the FTIR technique. Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectra of the oPD CD with characteristic absorption bands at 3429 cm−1 and 3167 cm−1, showing the presence of O-H stretching (broad, typically associated with alcohols and phenols) and N-H stretching (amines, amides; often seen in secondary amines). The presence of 1640 cm−1, 1489 cm−1, 1465 cm−1, 1405 cm−1, 1335 cm−1, 1221 cm−1, and 1055 cm−1 absorption bands reveal the existence of CC stretching (alkenes), CO stretching, C-H bending (aromatic compounds), CH₂ bending (methylene groups) and C-N stretching (amines). The presence of the characteristic functional groups, particularly the N-H and O-H groups, can undergo redox reactions [25], which is one of the applications utilized in this study. Fig. 2c depicts the elemental composition of the oPD CD collected using the SEM-EDX technique. Based on the nature of the starting material, the representative SEM-EDX spectrum reveals that carbon is the most abundant element in the oPD CD, constituting 63 % of the oPD CDs. This is followed by oxygen at 21.1 % and nitrogen at 16 %.

Fig. 3 presents the optical characteristics of the oPD CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra shown in Fig. 3a (orange line; orange axes) identify an absorption peak of 290 nm and 420 nm, which correspond to π-π* electronic transitions, which are associated with the conjugated system and aromatic compounds, as well as the n-π* electronic transition, which occurs in molecules with lone pairs of electrons, such as carbonyl compounds [26]. Unreacted o-phenylenediamine precursors typically exhibit characteristic absorption peaks in the UV region, specifically around 219 and 240 nm [27], distinct from the absorption profile of oPD CDs. In this study, the UV-Vis spectrum of the purified CDs showed no significant peaks corresponding to free o-phenylenediamine, confirming the successful removal of unreacted precursors.

It can be seen in Fig. 3a (blue dotted/line; blue axes) that the fluorescence spectra of the oPD CDs have a maximum excitation (dotted line) and emission (regular line) peak at 419 nm and 570 nm, respectively. Nitrogen-rich molecules like o-phenylenediamine are commonly used to red-shift carbon dots with fluorescence profiles from UV to violet light spectrum [28]. However, depending on the mode and conditions of synthesis, o-phenylenediamine can be used to obtain carbon dots with fluorescence wavelength above the violet fluorescence region, as seen in the work. Excitation wavelength-independent emission fluorescence offers advantages that make it a valuable tool in various scientific and technological applications, including improved signal stability, enhanced imaging capabilities, reliable sensing, and diagnostics [29]. The excitation wavelength-independent emission fluorescence of oPD CD is presented in Fig. 3b, where wavelength scan ranging from 420 nm to 520 nm did not influence the emission wavelength of the carbon dots; however, the fluorescence intensity reduced as the excitation wavelength increased, which can be associated with the reduction of the photon’s energy. The oPD CDs also show visible fluorescence when excited with ultraviolet light (SI Fig. 1b). Excitation wavelength-independent emission, commonly observed in specific carbon dots, arises from their unique optical and structural properties [30]. In the case of the oPD CDs synthesized in this study, the functional groups on the surface of the CDs, such as amino and hydroxyl groups, as identified by FTIR and SEM-EDX analyses, play a critical role in determining their optical properties. These groups create localized states that dominate the photoluminescence emission. The presence of such surface states allows the CDs to emit light at a fixed wavelength, independent of the excitation source, by decoupling the emission process from the intrinsic bandgap of the core. Moreover, excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior could be attributed to uniform particle size distribution, resulting in less heterogeneity in the electronic state of the surface chemistry of the oPD CDs. The quantum yield was 14 %, using rhodamine 110 as the reference fluorophore, while the quantitative yield was 72 % from a starting material of 25 mg of o-phenylenediamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH sensitivity and iron selectivity of oPD CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess how the fluorescence of the CDs behaves in response to different pH, an equal volume of oPD CDs was mixed with the Britton-Robinson (BR) universal buffer. As shown in Fig. 4a and SI Fig. 2a, the intensity of the carbon dots was quenched at acidic pH. However, as the pH reaches a neutral state, the oPD CDs fluorescence intensity is restored and stabilizes at the basic pH. According to the several mechanisms proposed [31], the modulation of the oPD fluorescence of the CDs at different pH could be subjective to pH-induced electron transfer. The selective test against selected metals and amino acids in 5 % DMSO shows that the oxidized form of iron (Fe3+) also significantly quenched the oPD CDs. DMSO is an aprotic polar solvent that coordinates with Fe³⁺ ions, stabilizing them in solution. This coordination reduces the interaction of Fe³⁺ in an aqueous solution, thereby preventing hydrolysis and subsequent Fe(OH)₃ formation [32]. However, the reduced form of iron (Fe2+) did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots, as presented in Fig. 4b and SI Fig. 2b. The functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots are vulnerable to protonation and deprotonation, thus changing the electronic configuration of the chemical structure of the carbon dots. Having detected the selectivity of the oPD CDs to the ferric ion, a relative fluorescence-concentration curve was examined with Fe3+ concentration range of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 μM. As shown in Fig. 4c, there was a linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Fe3+. The inset equation shows a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9708, and the detection limit was calculated to be 18μM using the Eqn 2.

A biocompatibility study was conducted at different carbon dot concentrations and incubation times for the carbon dot to be used for the cell culture experiments. Biocompatibility is one of the advantages of carbon dots nanoparticles, and as a result, they are used for real-time cell imaging [33]. After treating the cells with varying concentrations of the carbon dots, the cells were stained with propidium iodide (PI) to detect the dead cells. Flow cytometry was used to analyze the intensity of the penetrated PI dye in the cell. The more toxicity there is, the more intensity of penetrated PI in the cells.SI Fig. 3 shows the cell density chart obtained from the flow cytometer. A quadrant gating method was used on the cell density graph type and axis FSC-H against the PI axis. Q3 represents the region with dead cells, and the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) of Q3 is plotted in a graph as presented in Fig. 5a. The cells treated with 0.045, 0.09, and 0.18 mg/ml concentrations did not absorb PI as their MFI is not significantly different compared to the untreated control cells. Thus, concentrations of 0.045, 0.09, and 0.18 mg/ml are not toxic. However, 0.45 and 0.9 mg/ml concentrations of oPD CDs show significant toxicity compared with the control. However, the MFI of PI in the cells treated with ethanol was higher than all the concentrations of oPD CDs used. Because 30 min of incubation of 0.09 mg/ml was not toxic to the cells, we examined how the higher incubation duration (1, 2, 4, and 6 h) of 0.09 mg/ml oPD CDs in cells. SI Fig. 4 represents the histogram obtained from the flow cytometry analysis of the PI intensity absorbed by the cell. Fig. 5b shows the MFI of the PI, which correlates to the extent of cytotoxicity exerted by the oPD CDs at a particular time. The data shows that the 1-h incubation is the ideal incubation time for the oPD CDs not to be toxic. Although incubating for a longer time tends to induce cytotoxicity, the cells treated with ethanol pose more significant toxicity to the cells when compared with the untreated cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular Imaging and in-vitro iron overload detection
ce:section.ce:para: A prominent application of carbon dot nanoparticles is cellular imaging. After ascertaining the toxicity of the oPD CDs, their bioimaging application was examined. Fig. 6a and SI Fig. 5 show the fluorescence images obtained using blue filter cubes and other filters, UV (SI Fig. 5a), green (SI Fig. 5b), and red (SI Fig. 5c). The images showed that the oPD CDs were visible in the cell after 30 min of incubation. However, the carbon dots nanoparticles have the most fluorescence intensity when excited with the blue filter cubes. The merged image of the four filter cubes, Fig. 6b, shows that the green fluorescence is more conspicuous when compared to other emitted fluorescence. The image profile plot, Fig. 6c, conducted using ImageJ software, shows that the blue filter excites carbon dot nanoparticles to generate more photons, as the mean gray values were higher when compared with other filters.

Dysregulated iron metabolism usually results in iron overload, a condition associated with ferroptosis [34]. In this study, oPD CDs were used to detect ferrous ammonium sulfate (FAS)-induced iron overload in the selected leukemia cells. The cell utilizes iron in its reduced state (Fe2+, and ferritin stores excess iron in its oxidized state Fe3+ [35]. HL 60 and Jurkat cells were cultured with 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μM of FAS-supplemented media for 6 h before staining with oPD CDs for 30 min. Fig. 7a shows the mean gray value, which represents the intensity of the internalized oPD CDs nanoparticle of a minimum of 100 cells obtained from the fluorescence microscopic images of each treatment group, as compared to the cells stained with just oPD CDs, a significant reduction in the mean gray value cells exposed to FAS. Notably, the mean gray value of the cells treated with the highest concentration of FAS was significantly high, which we eventually postulated to be due to the cell's death. In consonance with the microscopic data, the flow cytometry analysis of the cells also shows a reduction in the MFI of the cells treated with FAS (Fig. 7b). The phenomena surrounding cell death are associated with generating reactive oxygen species [36]. These ROS interact with the surface functional groups of carbon dots, influencing their fluorescence properties [37].

As presented in Fig. 7c, the Jurkat cells also elicited a dose-dependent response in the MFI of the absorbed oPD CDs that were treated with varying concentrations of FAS. In contrast to HL 60 cells, where 400μM concentration of FAS causes increased MFI of the absorbed oPD CDs, Jurkat cells tend to maintain the downtrend of the MFI of the oPD CDs as the FAS concentration increases to 400μM. The difference in the oPD CDs fluorescence profile in the leukemia cell lines (HL-60 and Jurkat) can be attributed to the insusceptibility of Jurkat cells to ferroptosis compared to HL-60 cells [38]. Considering that 100 μM FAS elicited more response to the oPD CDs, HL 60 cells were cultured in 100μM FAS-supplemented media for 1, 2, 4, 6, and 24 h to examine how exposure time influences the detection of iron by oPD CDs. As shown in Fig. 7d, the oPD CDs response to FAS begins as early as 1 h, and at 6 h, a significant reduction in the mean gray value of the cells was documented. Although the mean gray value at 24 h was not statistically significant from the group treated with only carbon dot nanoparticles, the mean value increased when compared to 6 h of incubation. The microscopy image of oPD CD-treated HL 60 cells incubated without and with 100μM FAS is represented in Figs. 8a and 8b, respectively. Fig.ure 8c presents the image profile analysis using ImageJ software to produce a profile plot depicted in Figs. 8a and 8b.

To examine if oPD CDs possess fluorescence response to cell death as seen in 400 μM FAS treated cells and 24 h incubation, HL 60 cells were incubated with varying ethanol concentrations for 1 h. The change in fluorescence intensity of oPD CDs in dead cells further confirmed their cell death-sensing ability. As shown in Fig. 9a, 50 % and 75 % ethanol significantly increased the MFI of the absorbed oPD CDs. The image profile plot of the microscope image (SI Fig. 6) in Fig. 9b also corroborates the carbon dot nanoparticle's increased fluorescence response to increasing ethanol concentration.

In summary, a more advanced biosensing application of o phenylenediamine carbon dots was studied beyond pH application and bioimaging. As observed from the iron selectivity experiment, the oPD CDs were responsive to the ferric ion rather than the ferrous ion. Based on the relationship between redox and pH, the ferric ion is in the oxidized form of iron and tends to occur in low-pH conditions. It is not far-fetched that the synchronism between the effect of low pH on the fluorescence of the oPD CDs aligns with what was observed in the presence of ferric ions. A limitation of this study is that we were unable to test all possible interferants, and it is possible that an untested compound may elicit a similar or greater luminescence response. Though the oPD CDs synthesized in this work show a similar Fe3+-sensing activity with the references in Table 1, the carbon dots synthesized solely from o-phenylenediamine in Du et al. [16] using a similar approach as this current was not explored for their in vitro sensing ability. Hence, for the first time, the study shows the application of oPD CDs synthesized by facile and rapid microwave methods in detecting FAS-induced iron overload and ethanol-induced cell death. These studies emphasize the promise of oPD CDs for real-time iron detection and cellular imaging while shedding light on iron metabolism mechanisms in leukemia.

The manuscript was written with contributions from both authors. H.O.F. designed the experimental protocols and methodology, conducted the experiments, analyzed data, and wrote the draft of the paper. D.P. secured funding, designed the experiments, analyzed data, and edited the manuscript. Raw data is available upon request from the authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nxmate.2025.100667.
